name: Cameros Abandoned Villages Loop
name_original: Soto en Cameros, Torremuña – circular por pueblo abandonado
description: This difficult 32.4-mile mountain biking route near Soto en Cameros features abandoned villages and 3670 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Mountain bike trails around Soto En Cameros offer a rugged and diverse landscape within Spain's La Rioja region. The area is characterized by the dramatic Leza River Canyon, the mountainous Sierra de Cameros, and varied forest environments including the Monte Real beech forest. Riders encounter challenging terrain with abundant rocks and demanding singletracks, alongside historical elements like abandoned villages and Icnitas (dinosaur footprints). This region provides a less-explored mountain biking experience with a network of routes suitable for various skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Soto En Cameros The most popular mountain bike trail is Cameros and Iregua Valley Loop, a 30.5 miles (49.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 50 minutes to complete. This challenging route explores the rugged landscapes of the Cameros and Iregua valleys. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Ribafrecha - Route of the Hermitages Loop, a moderate 19.0 miles (30.6 km) path. This route offers a mix of natural scenery and historical sites, including hermitage chapels. Local mountain bikers also love the Clavijo Castle and Nalda Loop from Albelda de Iregua, a 21.9 miles (35.2 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Cameros, often completed in about 3 hours 58 minutes. Mountain biking around Soto En Cameros is defined by the Leza River Canyon, dense beech forests, and historic abandoned villages. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Soto En Cameros are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 500 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Soto En Cameros's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in Soto En Cameros?
A: There are over 40 mountain bike trails around Soto En Cameros, offering a diverse range of experiences. This includes 1 easy route, 21 moderate routes, and 21 difficult routes, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in Soto En Cameros?
A: The terrain in Soto En Cameros is known for its rugged relief within the Sierra de Cameros. You can expect challenging conditions with abundant rocks and demanding singletracks, especially on routes described as 'very agro'. The trails often wind through beech forests, ravines, and past abandoned villages, providing a varied and engaging ride.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails in Soto En Cameros?
A: While many trails are challenging, there is at least one easy mountain bike route available in Soto En Cameros. For families or beginners, it's advisable to check the difficulty ratings on komoot to find routes that match your group's skill level. The region's diverse landscape means there are options for less intense rides.

Q: What are some notable natural features or viewpoints I can see while mountain biking?
A: Mountain biking in Soto En Cameros offers stunning natural sights. You can experience the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3753514" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leza Canyon Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views of the Leza River Canyon. The region also features diverse forest environments like the Monte Real beech forest, and unique historical elements such as Icnitas (dinosaur footprints) near the canyon and Zenzano.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Soto En Cameros?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes in Soto En Cameros are circular. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993654724/cameros-and-iregua-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cameros and Iregua Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993654976/ribafrecha-route-of-the-hermitages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribafrecha - Route of the Hermitages Loop&lt;/a&gt; are both circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Soto En Cameros?
A: The mountain bike trails in Soto En Cameros are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 20 reviews. Riders often praise the 'epic' and 'flow' trails, the challenging singletracks, and the unique experience of riding through abandoned villages and diverse natural landscapes.

Q: Can I find mountain bike routes that visit historical sites or abandoned villages?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history, and many mountain bike routes incorporate visits to historical sites and abandoned villages. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993654428/cameros-abandoned-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cameros Abandoned Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically takes you through these intriguing locations, adding an adventurous and historical dimension to your ride.

Q: What are some other attractions near the mountain bike trails in Soto En Cameros?
A: Beyond the trails, you can explore several attractions. The historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2706737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clavijo Castle&lt;/a&gt; is a notable landmark. You can also visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3033682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of the Monastery of San Prudencio&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy the charm of traditional architecture in nearby villages like San Román de Cameros.

Q: Are there any specific bike parks or dedicated mountain biking areas nearby?
A: Yes, there are dedicated bike park areas around Soto En Cameros. You can explore trails like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4069664" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Resentida Trail&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2232128" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro 1800 Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which are part of nearby bike parks and offer specific downhill or technical riding experiences.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Soto En Cameros?
A: The Monte Real beech forest in Ajamil provides cool, shady routes in summer, making it ideal for riding during warmer months. In autumn, the same forests offer a vibrant display of colors. Given the 'rugged relief' and potential for demanding singletracks, spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for exploring the trails, while summer can be enjoyed in shaded areas.

Q: Are there any long-distance mountain bike routes for experienced riders?
A: Experienced riders seeking longer, more challenging routes will find several options. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993655323/ribafrecha-dehesa-de-san-roman-en-cameros-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribafrecha, Dehesa de San Román en Cameros – circular&lt;/a&gt; route is a difficult 58.4 km trail, typically taking over 5 hours to complete, offering extensive exploration of the region's demanding terrain.

Q: Is Soto En Cameros part of any protected natural areas?
A: Yes, Soto En Cameros is situated within the Biosphere Reserve of the Jubera, Leza, Cidacos, and Alhama valleys. This designation underscores the significant ecological value of the area, meaning you'll be riding through a well-preserved natural environment with diverse flora and fauna.
tips: The vulture viewpoint is located on the Cidacos greenway, a short distance from Arnedillo. From here you can see the rock that is just opposite, on the other side of the road and where you can see all the activity of the colony of vultures that reside here. Around 200 pairs live and fly in this area. You can see the nesting areas between the holes in the rocks.

San Román is the ideal base for any activity in the Leza Valley. It is the largest town in Camero Viejo and the one with the most services. Although there is only one bar/restaurant, Montereal, it is always open and you can taste typical Rioja dishes.

Its municipal term covers several towns and its paths are very well preserved. The houses around the church and the church itself are very well preserved. There are hardly any modern structures and a walk through its streets takes you back in time and to the traditional Rioja popular architecture.

This section through the Torremuña ravine is very characteristic. The path overlooks the stream passing over huge plates of strata discovered by erosion. The entire path alternates between shades of gray, ocher or orange layers. It does not have much of a slope but there are some small steps and the route alternates between the plates of the strata and the sediment accumulated over time.

In the 18th century, it is known that Torremuña produced cereals, legumes and wool yarn, in addition to having huge pastures. The church of Santa María la Blanca had its splendor in the 12th century when it was acquired by the bishops of Calahorra. Depopulation was reducing the town and in 1971 three Spanish-Flemish panels from the 16th century were stolen from its church.

The last census of 2010 registered 10 inhabitants, the times that I have passed through the town recently I have only found an elderly couple who are amazed to see a group pass by by bike, we always stop to contemplate the ruins of the church and the views of the valley of Leza.

La Santa is a beautiful abandoned town located in the Jubera river valley, in the province of La Rioja Media. The lands and houses were being abandoned as time passed and its population went from being 200 inhabitants in 1880 to having none in 1980.

faqs.question: How challenging is the mountain biking route Soto en Cameros, Torremuña – circular por pueblo abandonado?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult, requiring a very good physical condition and significant technical skill. It's ideal for experienced riders who enjoy Trail, All Mountain, and Enduro styles, with a technical difficulty rated 5.0 on the Single Trail Scale (STS).

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly rocky and hard, featuring strong gradients, abrupt ravines, and some forested sections. You'll encounter technical, rocky singletracks and some sections can be muddy depending on the season.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers stunning views of the Leza River Canyon Viewpoint and passes through the intriguing La Santa Abandoned Village, as well as Torremuña. You'll also pass by the San Román de Cameros Church and Historic Center.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this trail?
faqs.answer: While specific months aren't mentioned, consider that some sections can be muddy depending on the season. Generally, spring and autumn offer more moderate temperatures and potentially drier conditions for mountain biking in this region.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead for this route?
faqs.answer: The trail starts in Soto en Cameros. While specific parking details aren't provided, you would typically look for parking within the village to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this mountain biking trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The information available does not specify if dogs are allowed or if there are leash rules. It's always best to check with local regulations or park authorities in the Sierras Riojanas Biosphere Reserve before bringing your dog.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail in Sierras Riojanas?
faqs.answer: The trail is located within the Sierras Riojanas Biosphere Reserve. There is no mention of specific permits or entrance fees required for mountain biking this particular route. However, always respect the natural environment and any local regulations for protected areas.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: This mountain biking route typically takes around 4 hours and 22 minutes to complete, covering a distance of approximately 52 kilometers.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The area is known for its special ecological value. Keep an eye out for griffon vultures and eagles soaring above the Leza River Canyon, which are common sights in this Biosphere Reserve.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notable sections include parts of the Sierras Riojanas trail, Camino de Soto a Santa Marina, Camino a Santa Marina, and Camino Zarzosa-Jalón de Cameros, among others.

faqs.question: What level of experience and equipment do I need for this trail?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' classification and technical rating, this trail is best suited for experienced mountain bikers with a very good physical condition and significant technical skills. Riders should be comfortable with Trail, All Mountain, and Enduro styles, and have appropriate equipment for rocky, challenging terrain.
